props.first: Here's what the research actually shows: 49% of autistic 4-5 year olds are not yet toilet trained.
paras: That's not a made-up number. That's from a CDC-funded study of 743 children (Wiggins et al. 2022). Compare that to 8% of typically developing kids of the same age, and the gap is 6:1.
Thisn't happening because you didn't try hard enough. It's happening because the methods assume something that isn't true for your child: that they can feel when they need to go.
Whether your child is 3 or 10, verbal or non-verbal, newly diagnosed or still figuring it out, if they don't feel the "I need to go" signal, every method that assumes they do will struggle.

Every sticker chart and timer sit assumes your child feels the sensation of a full bladder. But research consistently shows many autistic children have a measurable difference in interoception — the eighth sensory system that tells the brain when we're hungry, thirsty, or need the bathroom.

The signal from bladder to brain is delayed or absent. It's not defiance. It's neurology. And here's the part that matters: interoception can be trained. The childhood window is the intervention opportunity.
The 3-Layer System:

1. Cotton Inner Layer — Creates a gentle, sustained "uh-oh" sensation when wet. Not a shock, not an alarm. A 30-60 second window of feedback that gives a nervous system that picks up signals slowly time to register: something happened.

2. Smart Absorption Core — Protects furniture, floors, and clothes without wicking the wetness signal away like a pull-up does. It's the difference between "too dry to feel" and "there's a signal your brain can learn from."
3. Leak-Resistant Outer Barrier — Made for real life. Playgrounds. Car seats. Grandma's couch. Because you shouldn't have to choose between body awareness and mess-free days.
Thisn't a pull-up replacement. It's a learning tool, designed for the brain that learns differently.

components.notes: Combines Validation (49% stat context), Mechanism (interoception), and 3-Layer System explainer into one Letter with 10 paras. Mechanism appears before Guarantee/CTA. 3-layer explainer rendered as numbered list inside the Letter.

props.first: "He said 'I need to pee' AFTER it's already happened for two years. After two weeks in BrightKidCo underwear, he paused. Then he looked down. Then he walked to the potty. It was small, maybe nothing to someone else. But that never happened before."
paras: , Mom of a 5-year-old, Level 2

"I was skeptical. We'd tried everything. But the 60-day guarantee made me feel safe trying. Week three, she stopped in the middle of playing and touched her underwear. She didn't make it to the bathroom that time. But she FELT it."
, Mom of a 4-year-old, Level 1
"In 9 years, he never once showed awareness of being wet. After a month in these, he looked down. He paused. That was huge for us."
, Mom of a 9-year-old, Level 3
BrightKidCo isn't training underwear in the traditional sense. It's a Body-Signal Learning System built around one principle: the brain needs feedback to learn.
60 days. By your judgment.

If it doesn't help your child make progress, if you don't see a pause, a look down, a single step toward the bathroom, full refund. No forms. No "did you try hard enough." Your judgment is the only metric that matters.
Start with the 3+3 Bundle, €79.99
6 pairs of Body-Signal Learning underwear. Free shipping. 60-day guarantee.
Whether you're just starting to explore or you've been at this for years, the bundle covers enough pairs to rotate through laundry cycles while your child's brain builds the connection.
Or start with 1 pair (€34.99) if you want to try before committing.
That's €13.33 per pair with the bundle — less than half the single-pair price.
Next time: How the Body-Signal Learning Layer works — and why it's different from everything you've tried.

variance_bypass: E2 non-bookend primitives: [CTAClose, FailedMethodsGrid, FramedImage, Letter, MechanismDef, PartBadge, Signoff, TitleBlock]. E3 must use several of these to satisfy other plan rules: (1) Signoff is the only primitive that reads from window.FLOW_WELCOME_GF_SHARED.signoff and is the canonical Lena card — required for E2+ signoff_name 'Lena Bauer'; (2) PartBadge is required by plan rule min_tilted_stickers>=1; (3) Letter is the only primitive that carries paragraph stacks (the 3-layer explainer, 3 testimonials, guarantee paras, and PS line all need paragraph text); (4) TitleBlock is the canonical hero with gradient accent (plan chunking guide: Hook -> TitleBlock); (5) FramedImage is the only primitive with hard-shadow prop and is required by plan rule min_hard_shadows>=1 and the 3-family triptych structure. Avoiding these would require redesigning the primitive catalog itself. The E3 composition is still structurally distinct from E2: E3 uses a 3-family FramedImage triptych (3 separate cards) instead of E2's FailedMethodsGrid; E3 uses a stat callout FramedImage (ImgFrame placeholder) that E2 lacks; E3 uses 2 Letters (combined content) vs E2's distributed Letter+MechanismDef+CTAClose pattern; E3 has 4 image slots vs E2's 3; E3 component count is 12 vs E2's 14. No new primitive types are introduced beyond what E2 used.
